![ads icon](/styles/img/transparent_logo.svg)

# **ads**

### view

## ADS

## Extending Kervaire invariant methods in stable homotopy theory

#### Abstract

This proposal is about extending the methods that the PI and his coauthors (M. A. Hill and M. J. Hopkins) developed and used to establish the nonexistence of elements in the stable homotopy groups of spheres with Kervaire invariant one, thereby solving a 50 year old problem in algebraic topology. The conclusion of their main theorem is the opposite of what experts in the field were hoping for in 1970s. For this reason the theorem raises as many questions as it answers.Algebraic topology is a collection of tools for studying problems in higher dimensional geometry. While the ideas are abstract, they have helped scientists understand the characteristics of electrons and other elementary particles and provided ideas used a few years ago to solve one of mathematics' most challenging problems, Fermat's Last Theorem. It is useful for understanding knots and robotics. Physicists use algebraic topology to try to figure out the shape of the universe. It helps them understand what they can observe and gives them tools to imagine what they cannot observe. Its ideas are also central to "string theory," where physicists conceive of space as having not just three dimensions but 10 or even more. Topology gives us a way to deal with 10-dimensional objects without actually seeing and touching them.Project Outcomes: Algebraic topology is a branch of mathematics with applications to physics and data science. It gives us powerful tools for dealing with higher dimensionsal geometry and higher dimensional symmetry. In 2009 the PI, along with Mike Hill of UCLA and Mike Hopkins of Harvard, unexpectedly (to us) found the answer to a 50 year old question in the subject known as the Kervaire invariant problem. Our solution made heavy use of techniques involving symmetry, known within the field as equivariant stable homotopy theory. This was surprising because the problem was not originally conceived in such terms. The answer we found was surprising for another reason. The problem has to do with the possible existence of higher dimensional manifolds having certain very delicate properties. It has been known since 1969 that they can only exist in dimensions two less than a power of two Since 1984 it has has also been known that the first five of them (in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30 and 62) do exist. In the 1970s many topologists tried and failed to construct them in all such dimensions. We now know why they failed, because we proved such structures do NOT exist in dimensions 254 and higher. The case of dimension 126 is still open and very challenging. It is conceivable that our methods could be refined to show that this sixth object also does not exist, or that some entirely different methods could show that it does. This is the subject of ongoing research. This nonexistence result is the opposite of what most experts were hoping for. Its full ramifications are still far from being understood. This breakthrough has led to increased interest in equivariant methods among both experts and graduate students. It indicates that such methods can provide insight into geometric problems appearing at first glance not to involve any kind of symmetry. The PI is writing a book designed to make these methods more accessible. The manuscript now has over 600 pages and is still growing. Stay tuned. Last Modified: 01/23/2018 Submitted by: Douglas C Ravenel

# The Kervaire invariant problem

On May 30th 2024 seminar goers at Princeton University witnessed a thrilling moment. The mathematician [Zhouli Xu](https://sites.google.com/view/xuzhouli) of the University of California, LA, announced that, together with colleagues, he had sorted out the 126th dimension. Not in general, but in regards to a problem that has taunted mathematicians since the 1960s. The problem involves strange shapes and is called the *Kervaire invariant problem*, after the mathematician [Michel Kervaire](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michel_Kervaire).

This year Xu visited our neighbours at the Isaac Newton institute for Mathematical Sciences (INI) in Cambridge to take part in a research programme called [*Equivariant homotopy theory in Context*](https://www.newton.ac.uk/event/eht/)*.*At the INI he joined[Michael Hill](https://cse.umn.edu/math/mike-hill) of the University of Minnesota, who helped to provide the previous breakthrough in 2009.

We asked Hill and Xu to show us around the many dimensions, retracing some of the steps on the long, and sometimes arduous, journey towards a proof of the Kervaire invariant problem. These articles and podcast tell the story.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/2025-06/IMG_5644.jpg?h=f524ccac&itok=XBu8MG8G)

[Living proof: Hunting through higher dimensions with Zhouli Xu](/content/living-proof-hunting-through-higher-dimension-zhouli-xu) — In this episode of our *Maths on the Move* podcast, Zhouli tells us about the Kervaire invariant problem, how he finally cracked dimension 126, and also has some advice for budding mathematicians.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/2025-06/small_AdobeStock_56010077.jpg?h=2a479378&itok=QHLG9w2S)

[Hunting strange shapes in higher dimensions](/content/hunting-strange-shapes-higher-dimensions) — This two-part article gives in in-depth, yet accessible glimpse of the Kervaire invariant problem and how it came to be solved.

### Background reading

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/top_frontpage.png?h=1c7b55f3&itok=zQVfZoXv)

[Maths in a minute: Topology](/content/maths-minute-topology) — Find out what topology is all about in this brief introduction.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/2025-05/Bildschirmfoto%202025-05-23%20um%2016.21.49.png?h=d5f291ac&itok=RXCGQgKO)

[Maths in a minute: Invariants](/content/maths-minute-invariants) — Discover the importance of invariants in just a few words.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/2025-05/Hypersphere_coord.png?h=bed64a37&itok=ldUsYiaQ)

[The hypersphere in four dimensions](/content/hypersphere-in-4D) — Learn about a higher dimensional sphere. You can't visualsie it, but you can still do maths with it.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/2025-06/sphere_newletter.jpg?h=1c7b55f3&itok=oJfAb33q)

[Maths in a minute: The Poincaré conjecture](/content/maths-minute-poincare-conjecture) — Find out more about a problem in topology that haunted mathematicians for a hundred years.

![](/content/sites/plus.maths.org/files/styles/small_square/public/weird_colours_frontpage_0.png?itok=y6gpkvMl)

[Telescope topology](/content/telescope-topology) — Not long ago we reported on another newly solved problem in topology. It concerns mathematical structures that are also important in the Kervaire invariant problem: stable homotopy groups of spheres.

--- SOURCE 5: Mathematicians, Including Professor Zhouli Xu, Prove Dimension ... ---
URL: https://ww3.math.ucla.edu/mathematicians-including-professor-zhouli-xu-prove-dimension-126-contains-strangely-twisted-shapes/

SUMMARY:
Mathematicians, Including Professor Zhouli Xu, Prove Dimension 126 Contains Strangely Twisted Shapes Over the years, mathematicians found that the twisted shapes exist in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, and 62. In a paper posted online last December, Weinan Lin and Guozhen Wang of Fudan University in Shanghai, along with Zhouli Xu of the University of California, Los Angeles, proved that 126 is indeed one of the rare dimensions that can host these strangely twisted shapes.” The associated Kervaire invariant problem seeks to identify precisely those dimensions in which smooth framed manifolds with Kervaire invariant one exist. Zhouli Xu and his collaborators resolved the final open case of the Kervaire invariant problem by proving the existence of framed manifolds with Kervaire invariant one in dimension 126.

enriched_points.expanded_content: The resolution of the Kervaire Invariant Problem marks a pivotal moment in homotopy theory, offering new insights into the structure of smooth manifolds and their invariants.

Equivariant stable homotopy theory techniques were crucial for the breakthrough, revealing symmetries in higher dimensions that were previously overlooked and aiding in the understanding of manifold properties.
The majority of dimensions related to the Kervaire invariant problem have been successfully resolved, confirming the existence or nonexistence of certain framed manifolds in these dimensions.
Despite significant progress, dimension 126 remains an open case, presenting ongoing challenges and opportunities for future research in algebraic topology and manifold theory.
